entities.name: aṇu
entities.cite: aṇavaḥ sarvaśaktitvādbhedasaṃsargavṛttayaḥ — atoms possessing all powers

entities.name: śakti
entities.cite: sarvaśaktitvāt — omnipotent in the small, each carrying every power

entities.name: chāya
entities.cite: take on word-being as one parinama among siblings: chaya, atapa, tamas, sabda

entities.name: atapa
entities.cite: take on word-being as one parinama among siblings: chaya, atapa, tamas, sabda

entities.name: tamas
entities.cite: take on word-being as one parinama among siblings: chaya, atapa, tamas, sabda

entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: transform themselves as shadow, light, darkness and word

entities.name: śabdākhyāḥ
entities.cite: the atoms called 'word', a name conceding that wordhood is a functional designation, not a natural kind

entities.name: parināma
entities.cite: take on word-being as one parinama among siblings

axioms.name: parinamate
axioms.cite: take on word-being as one parinama among siblings: chaya, atapa, tamas, sabda

axioms.name: sarvasaktitvat
axioms.cite: sarva-saktitvat — omnipotent in the small, each carrying every power

axioms.name: pracyante
axioms.cite: pracyante abhranive, mass like clouds: scattered potency condensing into audible weather

axioms.name: functional_designation
axioms.cite: 'the atoms called word', a name conceding that wordhood is a functional designation, not a natural kind

denials.name: eka_dravya
denials.cite: clouds are aggregates with edges only from a distance, form without unity

denials.name: paryapta
denials.cite: a cloud of word-atoms has no principled oneness for the hearer's single grasp

claims.name: parinamate
claims.cite: transform themselves as shadow, light, darkness and word

claims.name: pracyante
claims.cite: the atoms called 'word' accumulate like clouds

rejected_readings.label: unified_word_substance
rejected_readings.rendering: The atoms combine into a single, unified word-substance that hearer can grasp as one.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly denies this: clouds are 'aggregates with edges only from a distance, form without unity' and 'a cloud of word-atoms has no principled oneness for the hearer's single grasp'. The theory fails precisely because it cannot deliver the unity word requires.

rejected_readings.label: eternal_word_property
rejected_readings.rendering: Atoms eternally possess wordhood as an intrinsic property rather than generating it through transformation.
rejected_readings.why: The verse uses parinamirah (transform), not dharana (inherently possess). The commentary says atoms 'take on word-being as one parinama among siblings', stressing this is a transformation into one mode among others (shadow, light, darkness), not an eternal property. The functional designation ('a name conceding that wordhood is a functional designation, not a natural kind') is generated, not pre-existing.

rejected_readings.label: consciousness_orchestrates
rejected_readings.rendering: A conscious agent directs the effort by which atom-clouds condense into articulate words.
rejected_readings.why: The verse treats prayatna as a force property of the atoms themselves ('When their own power is being manifested, impelled by effort'), not as conscious direction from a speaker. The atomistic theory attributes word-generation to mechanical effort within nature, not intentional acts of agency. Introducing consciousness would solve the unity problem the commentary identifies—but the purvapaksa theory explicitly denies it, and that denial is part of what makes it a coherent (if incomplete) position.

rejected_readings.label: atoms_unified_like_vayu
rejected_readings.rendering: Like the vayu theory, atoms form a continuous medium that carries wordhood through the air.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly denies this: the commentary contrasts this atom-theory with 'the vayu theory [which] made word a *state* of one continuous stuff', and says 'this view grinds the substrate finer: anavah, atoms'. The atomistic theory uses scattered particles (prakiya-dravya), not a continuous medium like air. The cloud simile emphasizes aggregation of discrete units, not a single flowing substance.

notes: The verse presents the atomistic alternative to both Bhartrhari's own sphoṭa doctrine and the vayu theory of 1.108-109. The contract captures the theory's core claims: atoms are fundamental (omnipotent in the small), transform into various manifestation-modes (shadow, light, darkness, word), and accumulate like clouds under effort. The commentary's critique is encoded in the denials: the theory fails because word-atoms, like clouds, have 'no principled oneness' for the hearer to grasp as a single word. This contract presents what the purvapaksa theory commits to without endorsing it; Bhartrhari will later argue that such scattered aggregation cannot explain why the hearer understands a word as one unified meaning (1.84). The functional designation (sabda-akhya) itself registers the theory's self-awareness that 'wordhood' is a convenient label, not a natural kind.
